content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 8 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 104v–104r | **Source Images:** DEU_008_page_001.jpg, DEU_008_page_002.jpg, DEU_008_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** Every commandment I am giving you today you must be careful to follow — so that you may live and multiply and go in and take possession of the land the LORD swore to give your ancestors.

**2** Remember the whole journey the LORD your God led you on these forty years in the wilderness — humbling you, testing you, probing what was in your heart, to find out whether you would keep his commands or not.

**3** He humbled you by letting you go hungry, and then he fed you with manna — something neither you nor your ancestors had ever known — to teach you that human beings do not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD.

**4** The clothing on your back did not wear out and your feet did not swell these forty years.

**5** Know in your heart that just as a father disciplines his son, the LORD your God has been disciplining you.

**6** So keep the commands of the LORD your God: walk in his ways and hold him in reverent awe.

**7** For the LORD your God is bringing you into a good land — a land with flowing streams and springs and deep waters welling up in the valleys and hills;

**8** a land of wheat and barley, of vines and fig trees and pomegranates, a land of olive trees and honey;

**9** a land where you will eat food without scarcity, where you will lack nothing; a land whose stones are iron and from whose hills you can dig copper.

**10** When you have eaten your fill, you will bless the LORD your God for the good land he has given you.

**11** Be careful not to forget the LORD your God by failing to keep his commands and ordinances and statutes that I am giving you today.

**12** When you have eaten your fill and built fine houses and settled in them,

**13** and when your herds and flocks have multiplied and your silver and gold have increased and everything you have has grown more and more —

**14** then watch yourself, or your heart will become proud and you will forget the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.

**15** He led you through that vast and dreadful wilderness, a land of fiery snakes and scorpions, of parched and waterless ground. He brought water out of hard flint rock for you.

**16** He fed you in the wilderness with manna your ancestors had never known, humbling you and testing you so that in the end things would go well for you.

**17** You may say to yourself, "My own strength and the power of my own hands produced all this wealth for me."

**18** But remember the LORD your God, for it is he who gives you the power to produce wealth — in order to confirm his covenant which he swore to your ancestors, as it is today.

**19** If you ever forget the LORD your God and follow other gods and serve them and bow down to them, I warn you solemnly today that you will certainly perish.

**20** Like the nations the LORD is destroying before you, so you too will perish — because you would not obey the voice of the LORD your God.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 2 — "probing what was in your heart":** The Hebrew levedah et asher bilvavchem is literally "to know what was in your heart." PNEUMA uses "probing" — a more visceral, searching image — to capture the intensity of divine testing as genuine examination of interior life. The LXX has ekteirasai (to test fully), emphasizing exhaustive trial.

**V. 3 — The iconic "bread alone" verse:** Lo al ha-lechem levaddo yichyeh ha-adam ki al kol motsa fi-YHWH. PNEUMA renders this with deliberate plainness: "human beings do not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD." The word motsa (what comes out, outgoing) is rendered "word that comes from the mouth" rather than the more abstract "word" alone — preserving the breath-and-speech imagery. The LXX reads rhematos (utterance), which aligns with the oral, prophetic character of divine speech. This verse, famously quoted by Jesus in Matthew 4:4, carries enormous theological weight.

**V. 5 — "disciplines" for yasar:** The Hebrew yasar encompasses discipline, correction, chastening, and even physical punishment — all within a parental frame. PNEUMA keeps "disciplines" but translates the verse as a knowing, heart-level insight ("Know in your heart") rather than a mere command, because the Hebrew lachem da'at ("know in your heart") is reflective and personal.

**V. 7–9 — The "good land" as living poetry:** Moses' description of Canaan reads like a love poem about the land. PNEUMA preserves the catalogue structure — each "a land of..." phrase building on the last — because the cumulative rhythm creates a sense of overwhelming abundance. The LXX closely follows the Hebrew list. PNEUMA adds "deep waters welling up" for the Hebrew tehomot ("the deep") to evoke subterranean vitality rather than mere geological description.

**V. 10 — "bless the LORD":** Uverachta et YHWH Elohecha — this is the basis in Jewish law for birkat ha-mazon (grace after meals). PNEUMA translates the verbal imperative with the future form "you will bless" (following on "when you have eaten") to maintain the logical sequence rather than turning it into an isolated command.

**V. 14 — "your heart will become proud":** The Hebrew verom levavecha — literally "your heart will be lifted up." The metaphor of the rising heart as pride is well-established. PNEUMA uses "proud" as the most natural English equivalent, but emphasizes the physical-emotional image by keeping "heart" rather than using an abstract word like "attitude."

**V. 17–18 — The danger of self-attribution:** This is Moses' sharpest psychological diagnosis: the temptation to own one's prosperity. Kohi ve-otsem yadi asa li et ha-chayil ha-zeh — "my strength and the power of my hand made this wealth." PNEUMA renders "produced all this wealth" to capture the economic-achievement boast. The LXX uses ischys mou kai to kratos tes cheiros mou — the same muscular language. Verse 18's "power to produce wealth" (koach la'asot chayil) reminds that even human industriousness is a divine gift.

**V. 19–20 — Covenant warning as ethical urgency:** PNEUMA renders the double warning with "I warn you solemnly today that you will certainly perish" — unpacking the Hebrew's emphatic infinitive absolute (aved to'vedun) into English natural-language emphasis. The loss of the covenant is not presented as arbitrary punishment but as logical consequence: forgetting the LORD leads to the same fate as the nations who never knew him.

— Signed, PNEUMA
